problem: Characterizing how amino acid supplementation affects cell culture titer, glycosylation, and intracellular metabolism in hybridoma cell cultures.
method: Dynamic flux balance analysis (DFBA) using genome-scale mouse metabolic model combined with multivariate data analysis (PLS and PLS-DA).
finding: Media supplementation increases product yield by extending growth phase and raising nutrient levels; directed supplementation does not change overall cell metabolism, maintaining consistent product quality (glycoform distribution).
dataset: Murine hybridoma cell cultures (12 parallel cultures)
metric: Product yield, culture performance, glycoform assays
baseline: Previous metabolic models and cultures without supplementation
result: Increased cell density and extended growth phase with maintained metabolic state
enables: Enables rational optimization of biopharmaceutical manufacturing through metabolic engineering of antibody-producing cell lines.
limits: Limited to murine hybridoma system; does not address dynamic changes during entire culture lifecycle.
open_questions: Whether supplementation strategies generalize to human cell lines and other recombinant protein production systems remains unclear.

problem: Understanding three-dimensional cell morphogenesis and mechanical deformation in Arabidopsis pavement cells during leaf expansion.
method: 4D imaging framework combining direct water immersion observations, computational image segmentation, surface modeling, virtual reality visualization, and morphometric analysis.
finding: Cell morphology becomes increasingly complex during growth with quantifiable 3D shape changes; framework enables detailed phenotypic analysis and generates in silico cell structures for computational modeling.
dataset: Arabidopsis thaliana cotyledon pavement cells
metric: Cell growth rate, cell shape changes, cellular complexity over time
baseline: Previous 3D mathematical models and 2D microscopy approaches
result: Detailed 4D morphological tracking with increased cellular complexity quantified during growth
enables: Enables high-throughput phenotypic analysis of plant cell morphogenesis mutants and validation of computational simulations against experimental 3D data.
limits: Limited to plant cotyledon cells; requires specialized imaging equipment and computational resources.
open_questions: How mechanical forces and molecular signals integrate to drive the complex jigsaw puzzle-like cell shape changes remains partially understood.

problem: Evaluating the utility and challenges of applying mathematical and computational modeling approaches to understand Natural Killer (NK) cell biology and immune system function.
method: Survey of recent quantitative approaches and mathematical tools applied to NK cell biology investigations.
finding: Quantitative modeling approaches provide valuable insights into NK cell biology despite historical ambivalence in the biology community; computational tools increasingly recognized as important for addressing biological questions.
dataset: NK cell biology systems and immune system datasets
baseline: Previous empirical and qualitative biological studies
result: Comprehensive evaluation of quantitative tools' utility in NK cell research
enables: Enables integration of mathematical rigor into immunology research and supports development of predictive immune models.
limits: Review-based approach; does not provide specific quantitative benchmarks for method performance.
open_questions: How to best integrate diverse data types and scales for comprehensive NK cell system modeling remains an open challenge.

problem: Modeling and simulating cell cycle dynamics and cancer cell proliferation under defined growth conditions.
method: Agent-based modeling (ABM) simulator that reproduces dynamics of cancer cell population in exponential growth, validated against HCT116 colon cancer in vitro experiments.
finding: Model accurately predicts cell confluence effects on G1 phase elongation; nocodazole synchronization reveals unexpected effects on overall cell cycle progression.
dataset: HCT116 colon cancer cells
metric: Cell cycle phase durations, confluence-induced elongation
baseline: Experimental in vitro data from synchronized cancer cells
result: Model predictions validated against cell culture experiments
enables: Enables prediction of cancer cell proliferation dynamics and identification of novel nocodazole effects for therapeutic targeting.
limits: Limited to single cancer cell line; does not model heterogeneous cell populations or in vivo microenvironment.
open_questions: Whether checkpoint regulation mechanisms vary across different cancer types and how microenvironmental factors affect cell cycle progression in vivo remains unclear.

problem: Developing computational digital twin models for mammalian cell culture bioprocesses to advance biomanufacturing optimization.
method: Digital twin modeling approach (methodology details limited in available abstract).
dataset: Mammalian cell culture systems
enables: Enables real-time monitoring and optimization of biomanufacturing processes through digital representations of physical bioprocesses.
limits: Specific model limitations and validation scope not detailed in available information.
open_questions: How to integrate heterogeneous data streams and achieve real-time predictive accuracy across diverse bioprocess scales remains uncertain.

problem: Understanding cell-tissue communication dynamics in osteoarthritis by integrating chondrocyte regulation and mechanical properties of articular cartilage.
method: Multiscale finite-element model (FEM) coupled with reaction-diffusion equations; chondrocyte regulatory network model (RNM) restricted to cell surfaces in pericellular matrix; validated with in vitro cartilage degradation model.
finding: Diffusion streamlines reveal chondrocyte communication with varying gradients; initial phenotypes persist; pro-inflammatory chondrocyte activity matches experimental model after 2 weeks, supporting multiscale model integration validity.
dataset: 2D FEM cartilage plugs with distributed chondrocytes; in vitro knee articular cartilage explants (5 mm plugs, 3 young calves, 2 replicates)
metric: Gene expression (OA markers), tissue concentrations, chondrocyte activity
baseline: In vitro cartilage degradation experiments with IL-1b stimulation
result: Computational pro-inflammatory predictions matched experimental trends after 2 weeks
enables: Enables personalized osteoarthritis research and therapeutic targeting through patient-specific multiscale models incorporating mechanical and molecular dynamics.
limits: Limited to 2D geometry and in vitro validation; mechanical stimulation not yet incorporated; discrepancies found for MMP3, IL-6, ITGA5 markers.
open_questions: How mechanical forces integrate with chondrocyte signaling and whether the model extends to patient-specific synovial fluid concentrations remain open.

problem: Scaling whole-cell (WC) models from small bacteria to human cells that contain 100x more molecular species and 10,000-100,000x more molecules for medical applications.
method: Novel system for building and simulating WC models using sequence and rule-based modeling language with multi-algorithm simulator employing optimistic parallel discrete event simulation.
finding: Design framework presented for scalable WC modeling incorporating multiple mathematically distinct pathway sub-models into unified multi-algorithm simulations.
dataset: Human cells (general framework)
metric: Molecular species count, molecular abundance, simulation scalability
baseline: Existing whole-cell model of small bacterium (Mycoplasma genitalium)
result: Scalable architecture designed for orders-of-magnitude increase in model complexity
enables: Enables rational design of medical therapies and bioengineered microorganisms through comprehensive cellular phenotype prediction.
limits: Framework described but not fully implemented; human cell models not yet demonstrated.
open_questions: How to efficiently integrate thousands of pathway modules and maintain computational tractability for complex human cell models remains to be demonstrated.

problem: Elucidating mechanisms governing passage and retention of red blood cells (RBCs) through 0.3 µm splenic interendothelial slits without slit opening.
method: Multiscale modeling combined with live imaging and microfluidic experiments on devices with physiological slit dimensions; 2D Poiseuille flow analysis.
finding: Healthy RBCs pass through 0.28 µm rigid slits by deforming into tether-connected spheres and unfolding spectrin cytoskeleton; transit dynamics follow Newtonian fluid behavior; PIEZO1 activation not required; filtration possible without slit opening.
dataset: Red blood cells from healthy donors and patients; microfluidic slit devices with submicron widths
metric: RBC transit time, slit width, in-slit pressure drop, cytoplasmic viscosity
baseline: Previous hypotheses proposing slit opening during RBC passage
result: RBC transit time scales with pressure drop and slit width to the -1 and -3 power respectively
enables: Enables rational design of transfusion strategies and engineered RBC drug delivery through understanding of physiological RBC clearance mechanisms.
limits: In vitro microfluidic model; does not capture complex splenic flow patterns and immune interactions.
open_questions: Whether abnormal RBC mechanical properties associated with disease permit passage through physiological slits and how aging affects deformability remains unclear.

problem: Modeling multiscale dynamics of malaria-infected red blood cells.
method: Multiscale modeling approach (methodology details limited in available information).
dataset: Malaria-infected red blood cells
enables: Enables understanding of malaria pathophysiology and potential identification of therapeutic targets.
limits: Specific model details and validation evidence not provided.
open_questions: How parasite biology integrates with RBC mechanics at multiple scales remains under investigation.

problem: Modeling multiscale dynamics of malaria-infected red blood cells.
method: Multiscale modeling approach (methodology details limited in available information).
dataset: Malaria-infected red blood cells
enables: Enables understanding of malaria pathophysiology and potential identification of therapeutic targets.
limits: Specific model details and validation evidence not provided.
open_questions: How parasite biology integrates with RBC mechanics at multiple scales remains under investigation.

problem: Reducing computational expense and improving efficiency of whole-cell simulations for bacteria through modular cell biology principles.
method: Brownian dynamics-based parallel whole-cell simulation using Hamiltonian mechanics with velocity Verlet integration; collision detection and resolution algorithms; cellular dictionary data structure for subcell organization.
finding: CUDA C/C++ implementation achieves computational time reduction with increased computing cores (stable at ~128 cores); scalable to higher organisms (rats, humans); collision resolution enables accurate particle dynamics.
dataset: Escherichia coli, with extension to rats and humans
result: 95% computational time reduction achievable with GPU-CPU cluster; stable scaling at ~128 cores
enables: Enables high-performance whole-cell simulations for synthetic biology and systems biology applications across diverse organisms.
limits: Testing limited to computational benchmarks; biological validation against experimental data not detailed.
open_questions: Whether simulated subcellular dynamics accurately capture emergent cellular behaviors under diverse growth conditions remains to be validated experimentally.

problem: Accelerated stress testing and performance characterization of carbon-based gas diffusion layers for fuel cell applications.
method: Accelerated testing methodology applied to PUREBLACK® carbon-based gas diffusion layers with pore-forming agents (methodology details limited).
dataset: PUREBLACK® carbon-based gas diffusion layer materials for proton exchange membrane fuel cells
enables: Enables faster development and optimization of fuel cell components through accelerated durability testing.
limits: Full abstract and methodology not available.
open_questions: How pore-forming agent composition affects long-term fuel cell performance and durability remains to be determined.

problem: Improving battery cell manufacturing efficiency through multiscale and multiphysics modeling tools.
method: Accelerated multiscale and multiphysics modeling (methodology details limited).
dataset: Battery cell manufacturing processes
enables: Enables faster battery development cycles and improved manufacturing processes through computational modeling.
limits: Abstract information severely limited.
open_questions: Which physical phenomena most significantly impact manufacturing yield and performance remains unclear.

problem: Developing novel Schiff base ligands and transition metal complexes for cholesterol lowering and anticancer applications.
method: Chemical synthesis and characterization of Schiff base ligands and transition metal complexes; ADME analysis; computational studies; in vitro anticancer testing on HeLa cells.
dataset: HeLa cancer cells
enables: Enables development of dual-activity compounds targeting both cholesterol metabolism and cancer cell viability.
limits: Full abstract not provided; in vivo efficacy and specificity not addressed.
open_questions: Whether compounds show selective toxicity to cancer cells versus normal cells and how they affect cholesterol metabolism in vivo remains to be determined.

problem: Developing bioimaging agents for detection of iron (Fe3+) in live mammalian cells.
method: Design and synthesis of tQ[14]-based aggregation-induced emission (AIE) supramolecular network polymers (methodology details limited).
dataset: HeLa cancer cells
enables: Enables real-time monitoring of iron homeostasis and iron-related pathologies in live cells.
limits: Full methodology and quantitative results not provided.
open_questions: Whether the probe can differentiate between Fe2+ and Fe3+ in complex cellular environments remains unclear.

problem: Developing coordinated power control strategies with virtual inertia for fuel cell-based DC microgrids.
method: Power control methodology for coordinated operation across microgrids (methodology details limited).
dataset: Fuel cell-based DC microgrid systems
enables: Enables stable and efficient operation of distributed fuel cell systems in microgrid applications.
limits: Full abstract not available.
open_questions: How virtual inertia emulation affects system stability under variable load conditions remains to be characterized.

problem: Optimizing scheduling strategies for virtual power plants incorporating reversible solid oxide cells in electricity markets.
method: Optimization modeling for virtual power plant scheduling (methodology details limited).
dataset: Virtual power plant with reversible solid oxide cell (RSOC) components
enables: Enables efficient integration of flexible energy storage systems into electricity markets.
limits: Full abstract not provided.
open_questions: How market price volatility affects optimal scheduling and economic performance remains unclear.

problem: Developing high power density and efficient power conversion using multi-cell rectifier topologies with GaN transistors.
method: ISOS (Input Series Output Series) multi-cell rectifier topology with isolated dc-dc converters and open-loop controlled buck-boost converters; TCM (Triangular Current Mode) control with GaN power transistors.
finding: Open-loop controlled virtual capacitor converters achieve voltage sharing without sensors or feedback control; TCM control enables soft-switching with GaN transistors; prototype demonstrates >96% efficiency and 3.0 W/cm³ power density.
dataset: 100V AC 450W ISOS multi-cell rectifier prototype with three cell converters and two VCCs
baseline: Previous multi-cell rectifier topologies without virtual capacitor approach
result: Efficiency >96%, power density >3.0 W/cm³, successful open-loop voltage equalization
enables: Enables development of high-efficiency scalable power electronic transformers for industrial and renewable energy applications.
limits: Prototype tested at single power level; performance under variable load and temperature conditions not fully characterized.
open_questions: How open-loop control maintains stability across wide operating ranges and how the approach scales to higher voltage levels remains to be demonstrated.

problem: Accelerating whole-cell model (WCM) runtime to enable rapid exploration of genome design space for synthetic biology applications.
method: Machine learning surrogate models trained on WCM simulation data to predict cell division; ML surrogate combined with genome-design algorithm; validation via WCM; biological interpretation using Gene Ontology analysis.
finding: ML surrogate achieves 95% reduction in computational time compared to original WCM; surrogate and genome-design algorithm generate viable in silico E. coli with 40% gene deletion; Gene Ontology analysis validates biological interpretability.
dataset: Escherichia coli whole-cell model, refined genome designs
metric: Computational time reduction (%), genome reduction percentage, cell division prediction accuracy
baseline: Original whole-cell model without ML acceleration
result: 95% computational speedup; 40% gene deletion possible with functional viability
enables: Enables rational synthetic biology through rapid exploration of reduced genomes and strain design optimization.
limits: ML surrogate validated only for cell division prediction; does not model all cellular phenotypes; E. coli-specific.
open_questions: Whether ML surrogates trained on one WCM accurately predict phenotypes under novel environmental conditions and whether the approach extends to eukaryotic cells remains uncertain.

problem: Understanding how epigenetic heterogeneity drives pathological cell fate reprogramming and determining mechanisms controlling entry-exit from pluripotent-like states.
method: Stochastic multiscale model combining epigenetic regulation (ER) and gene regulatory networks (GRN); hybrid numerical simulation method for model reduction; stability analysis via dynamic systems theory.
finding: ER heterogeneity confers abnormal robustness to pluripotent stem-like states; system exhibits tristability with pluripotent, differentiated, and indecisive steady states; kinetic manipulation of epigenetic factors enables state transitions without DNA changes.
dataset: Theoretical model validated against literature findings in cell reprogramming
metric: Stability analysis, state transition kinetics, robustness parameters
baseline: Previous models without integrated ER-GRN coupling or heterogeneity
result: Identified epigenetic heterogeneity as driver of phenotypic robustness; demonstrated tunable switching mechanisms
enables: Enables development of therapeutic strategies for tissue regeneration and cancer treatment through epigenetic manipulation without genetic alterations.
limits: Theoretical model; limited experimental validation of predicted epigenetic switching strategies.
open_questions: Whether in vivo epigenetic manipulation can reliably induce desired cell fate transitions without off-target effects and whether findings generalize across cell types remains to be demonstrated.

problem: Understanding how mechanical, chemical, and biological factors interact across scales to regulate cell-substrate adhesion dynamics from molecular clustering to cellular migration.
method: Multiscale mechanobiochemical coupling framework integrating integrin life cycle (activation, clustering, signal transduction, internalization), caveolin-mediated trafficking, and actin-generated traction forces.
finding: Model identifies antagonistic interplay between integrin internalization and clustering governing cross-scale adhesion; substrate stiffness quantitatively regulates integrin clustering size and internalization rate; explains durotaxis and negative durotaxis transitions.
dataset: Cell-substrate adhesion systems with variable substrate stiffness
metric: Integrin clustering size, internalization rate, focal adhesion morphology, mechanical forces
baseline: Previous molecular and cellular adhesion models without multiscale mechanobiochemical coupling
result: Quantitative demonstration of stiffness-dependent integrin regulation; mechanistic explanation of durotaxis transitions
enables: Enables rational engineering of cell-matrix interactions for regenerative medicine and cancer therapeutics through substrate design.
limits: Model focuses on integrin mechanics; does not incorporate full complement of adhesion proteins or complex tissue-level interactions.
open_questions: How other integrin-binding ligands and mechanical transduction pathways interact with the described mechanisms and whether findings apply to in vivo matrix environments remains unclear.

problem: Overcoming computational bottleneck of intracellular biophysical simulations that include millions of component interactions for synthetic biology and basic research applications.
method: Dedicated hardware designed specifically for biophysical simulation; focus on mRNA translation as proof-of-concept; hardware simulates thousands of mRNAs and ribosomes in E. coli and S. cerevisiae.
finding: Dedicated hardware achieves orders-of-magnitude speedup (thousands of times faster) compared to software simulation of mRNA translation; approach applicable to whole-cell simulation.
dataset: mRNA translation systems in Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae
result: Orders-of-magnitude computational acceleration for translation simulation
enables: Enables intracellular biophysical simulations for synthetic biology design and whole-cell modeling applications.
limits: Hardware prototype demonstrated only for mRNA translation; scalability to full whole-cell simulation not yet proven; single-organism focus.
open_questions: Whether dedicated hardware can integrate diverse cellular processes simultaneously and how it handles heterogeneous scales within a single organism remains to be demonstrated.

problem: Predicting cell-type-specific and behavioral state-dependent neural dynamics in primary motor cortex by integrating molecular, cellular, circuit, and behavioral scales.
method: Biophysically detailed multiscale model of mouse M1 with 10,000+ neurons and 30 million synapses; parameters constrained by experimental data (morphology, connectivity, biophysics, synapse location); includes long-range inputs from 7 regions and noradrenergic modulation.
finding: Model accurately predicts layer- and cell-type-specific firing rates and local field potentials (LFP) associated with behavioral states and experimental manipulations (noradrenaline blockade, thalamus inactivation); generates mechanistic hypotheses and identifies low-dimensional population dynamics.
dataset: Mouse primary motor cortex (M1), in vivo recordings during quiet wakefulness and movement
metric: Firing rates, LFP, cell-type-specific responses, behavioral state classification
baseline: Experimental in vivo neural recordings from M1
result: Accurate prediction of layer- and cell-type-specific neural responses across behavioral states
enables: Enables mechanistic understanding of motor cortex function and rational prediction of neural responses to interventions for neuroscience and neuroengineering applications.
limits: Model specific to mouse M1; does not incorporate top-down cognitive factors; temporal resolution limited by integration timestep.
open_questions: Whether model generalizes to other cortical areas, species, and how learning-dependent plasticity affects multiscale dynamics remains to be investigated.

problem: Identifying gene alterations driving hematopoietic dysfunction in paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) patients with bone marrow failure through patient-derived iPSC modeling.
method: Patient-derived iPSC differentiation into hematopoietic stem/progenitor cells (HSPCs); PIGA mutation correction via CRISPR/Cas9; whole-genome sequencing (WGS) on PNH-iPSCs, normal iPSCs, and somatic cells; cross-reference with curated hematopoiesis gene databases.
finding: PNH-iPSC model reproduces hematopoietic dysfunction; PIGA correction alone insufficient to restore function, indicating additional pathogenic factors; WGS identified multiple clone-specific somatic mutations involving known genes (DNMT3A, ASXL1) and novel genes; no shared mutations across patients, supporting genetic heterogeneity.
dataset: Five PNH patients with bone marrow failure; patient-derived iPSCs and HSPCs
metric: Hematopoietic differentiation/proliferation rates, mutation count and type, gene-phenotype correlation
baseline: Wild-type iPSCs from same patients; normal hematopoiesis parameters
result: Reproduced hematopoietic dysfunction in vitro; identified patient-specific somatic mutations with potential phenotypic impact
enables: Enables precision medicine approaches for PNH-BMF treatment through patient-specific genetic profiling and therapeutic target identification.
limits: Limited sample size (5 patients); in vitro iPSC-derived HSPCs may not fully recapitulate in vivo bone marrow niche; knock-in validation ongoing.
open_questions: Whether identified novel mutations are directly pathogenic for hematopoietic dysfunction and whether correction strategies will achieve therapeutic benefit in vivo remain to be determined.

problem: Modeling three-dimensional human epidermal structure and barrier function using agent-based modeling with realistic cellular morphology.
method: Three-dimensional agent-based modeling approach incorporating realistic cell morphologies (methodology details limited in available information).
dataset: Human epidermis
enables: Enables computational prediction of skin barrier function and effects of perturbations on epidermal homeostasis.
limits: Full abstract not provided; validation and specific model features unclear.
open_questions: How well the 3D agent-based model captures realistic skin barrier function and whether it can predict effects of drugs or genetic mutations remains to be demonstrated.
